metadata.summary: Reduce LLM costs 60-90% through model routing, prompt caching, response caching, token reduction, and Batch API. Optimize only after measuring per-stage costs.

content.title: LLM Cost Optimization
content.summary: LLM costs can be reduced 60-90% through model routing (50-75% savings), prompt caching (80-90% on repeated system prompts), response caching (70-95% for deterministic queries), token reduction (20-40%), and Batch API (50% discount on async workloads). Track cost per pipeline stage from day 1 — you cannot optimize what you cannot attribute. Optimize only after measuring; premature optimization wastes time.

why.p: LLM costs scale super-linearly in production: each agent turn, each pipeline stage, each user query multiplies. Output tokens cost 3-5x more than input tokens. Without model routing, every request goes to the most expensive model by default. Without prompt caching, the same 2K-token system prompt is billed repeatedly. Budget runaway is possible within hours on high-traffic agentic pipelines.

list.item: Monthly LLM spend exceeds $500 and is growing faster than revenue
Agentic pipelines with greater than 10K requests/day where model selection matters
Batch workloads with greater than 1h latency tolerance (Batch API = 50% off)
System prompts greater than 1K tokens (OpenAI) or greater than 2K tokens (Claude) that repeat across many calls
Multi-step pipelines where early stages can use cheap models for routing
Early prototype phase — optimize only after measuring
Tasks where quality degradation from cheaper models is unacceptable (medical, legal, safety-critical)
Very low volume (less than 100 calls/day) — savings are negligible, complexity is not worth it
When the cost driver is output tokens on complex reasoning tasks — only better prompts help, not routing

section.name: model-routing
section.summary: Route tasks by complexity to different models, saving 50-75% by avoiding expensive models on simple tasks.

rule.statement: Route tasks by complexity: nano/Haiku for classification and routing, mini/Sonnet for standard queries, frontier model (gpt-4o/Opus) only for complex reasoning. Use a cheap classifier to route — even 5% misclassification is acceptable.
rule.rationale: Typical savings: 50-75%. The classifier itself costs tokens — ensure routing overhead is less than savings. At gpt-4o-mini ($0.15/M) vs gpt-4o ($2.50/M), routing 80% of traffic to mini saves ~$1.88 per 1M input tokens.

example.title: Complexity-based model router

section.name: prompt-caching
section.summary: Mark static content with cache_control to reuse across requests, saving 80-90% on input tokens for repeated system prompts.

rule.statement: Enable prompt caching for system prompts greater than 1024 tokens (OpenAI) or greater than 2048 tokens (Claude). Place static content first, dynamic content last. The prefix must match byte-for-byte — even a space invalidates the cache.
rule.rationale: Typical savings: 80-90% on input tokens for cached prefixes. Second and subsequent calls with the same system prompt pay only for cache read (much cheaper) not full input. Savings compound on high-volume pipelines.

example.title: Prompt caching with Claude
example.code:
import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic() SYSTEM = "You are an expert analyst. [... 2000+ token static context ...]"
response = client.messages.create( model="claude-sonnet-4-5", max_tokens=1024, system=[{"type": "text", "text": SYSTEM, "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}}], messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Analyze: ..."}], ) # Second call with same system prompt hits cache: 90% input cost reduction

section.name: response-caching
section.summary: Cache deterministic responses to avoid redundant API calls, saving 70-95% for repeated queries.

rule.statement: Cache deterministic responses (temperature=0) at exact match: cache key = hash(model + system_prompt + user_message). TTL 24h for most content, shorter for time-sensitive data. Never cache temperature greater than 0 responses.
rule.rationale: Typical savings: 70-95% for repeated queries. Users often ask the same question. Exact-match caching with Redis has less than 1ms lookup overhead vs 500ms+ API calls. Temperature greater than 0 caching returns stale creative outputs users notice.

section.name: token-reduction
section.summary: Set output token limits and trim verbose prompts to reduce billable token count by 20-40%.

rule.statement: Set max_tokens on every call as both a cost cap and a quality signal — if output truncates, the task is too broad and needs redesign. Remove verbose phrases from prompts but test that removal doesn't degrade output quality.
rule.rationale: Output tokens cost 3-5x more than input tokens. A hard max_tokens prevents runaway generation. "Please kindly" adds 3 tokens with no value — but aggressive removal of instructions can break models trained on verbose prompting.

section.name: batch-api
section.summary: Submit jobs for background processing with 50% cost discount for workloads tolerant of greater than 1 hour latency.

rule.statement: Use Batch API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) for workloads tolerant of greater than 1h latency: 50% discount, no rate limit pressure, JSONL input. Do NOT wait synchronously for batch results — use async polling or a completion-handler agent.
rule.rationale: Batch API submits jobs for background processing. The 50% discount applies to both input and output tokens with no quality difference. Ideal for: bulk classification, content generation, nightly analysis pipelines.

list.item: Model routing: 50-75% cost reduction, low implementation complexity
Prompt caching: 80-90% on repeated system prompts, medium complexity
Response caching: 70-95% for deterministic queries, medium complexity
Token reduction: 20-40%, low complexity
Batch API: 50% for async workloads, medium complexity
Combined: 60-90% total reduction

section.name: budget-control-rules
section.summary: Concrete guardrails to prevent cost runaway in production pipelines.

rule.statement: Set separate budget caps for non-critical (news summarization) vs. critical (user-facing answers) pipelines. Alert at 80% daily budget; disable non-critical calls at 100%. Never disable critical pipelines automatically.
rule.rationale: A single runaway non-critical pipeline should not take down user-facing features. Separate caps prevent cascade failures where one pipeline exhausts the shared budget.

rule.statement: Review the top-10 most expensive agent calls weekly and redesign them. Cost concentrates in a few high-frequency or high-output patterns.
rule.rationale: Pareto principle applies: 20% of call patterns generate 80% of cost. Weekly review makes cost optimization an ongoing practice rather than a one-time event.

section.name: monitoring-rules
section.summary: Instrumentation and observability for cost-per-stage attribution.

rule.statement: Track cost per pipeline stage from day 1 — not total cost. Use response.usage (OpenAI) or response.usage (Claude) to record input/output tokens per agent step.
rule.rationale: Total cost is unactionable. Stage-level attribution tells you which steps to optimize. Cost dashboards lag 15-60 minutes — real-time caps require client-side token counting, not API usage data.

rule.statement: Separately track prompt caching metrics and semantic caching metrics. They have different failure modes and different optimization levers.
rule.rationale: Prompt caching fails silently when the prefix changes. Semantic caching fails with false positives. Mixing them in a single "cache hit rate" metric obscures which layer is failing.

antipattern.name: semantic-cache-false-positives
antipattern.description: Semantic cache false positives: "what is the capital of France?" and "what is the capital of Germany?" may have cosine similarity greater than 0.95 and return the wrong cached answer.
antipattern.reason: Semantic caching threshold tuning requires offline evaluation on your actual query distribution. A threshold of 0.95 is often too aggressive — test with 0.98+ for factual queries.

antipattern.name: routing-classifier-overhead
antipattern.description: Routing classifier overhead exceeds savings on short tasks.
antipattern.reason: If the classifier takes 200 tokens to route a 50-token task, the overhead can exceed the savings from cheaper model selection. Only route when task token count significantly exceeds classification overhead.

antipattern.name: prompt-caching-prefix-invalidation
antipattern.description: Prompt caching invalidated by a single space change in the system prompt.
antipattern.reason: OpenAI and Claude require exact byte-for-byte prefix matches. Dynamic content injected into the static system prompt prefix (e.g., current date, user name) invalidates the cache on every call.

antipattern.name: budget-alert-timing
antipattern.description: Human-in-loop checkpoint: when daily budget hits 80%, alerting after the automatic shutdown has already corrupted pipeline state.
antipattern.reason: Alert at 80% to allow human intervention before shutdown. Shutdown mid-pipeline corrupts in-flight state. Design graceful shutdown: complete current steps, then pause new intake.

antipattern.name: response-caching-temperature
antipattern.description: Response caching with temperature greater than 0 returns stale creative outputs that users notice.
antipattern.reason: Temperature greater than 0 caching returns stale creative outputs users notice. Cache only at temperature=0.

antipattern.name: aggressive-token-reduction
antipattern.description: Aggressive token reduction (removing "please", verbose instructions) can break models trained on verbose instruction-following.
antipattern.reason: Token reduction saves 20-40%, but removing instructions can degrade output quality. Test that removal doesn't degrade output quality.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Agents apply cost optimization in two layers: routing (which model) and caching (avoid calling at all). A lightweight classifier agent (Haiku/gpt-4o-mini) reads the task complexity score and routes to Sonnet or Opus. Before any LLM call, a cache lookup checks Redis for exact match (temperature=0) or semantic match (embedding similarity greater than 0.95). The agent pipeline itself should be instrumented to emit per-step token counts, enabling offline analysis of which stages drive the most cost. Budget watchdog agents fire alerts at 80% of daily cap and kill non-critical calls at 100%.
Recommended subagents: Any routing agent using a nano/mini model as the classifier. faion-sdd-execution uses cheaper models for planning steps, Opus only for critical decisions.

list.item: Track cost per pipeline stage from day 1, not total cost — you can't optimize what you can't attribute
Use output token limits (max_tokens) as both a cost cap and a quality signal — if output truncates, the task is too broad
Classify tasks by complexity with a cheap model before routing; even 5% misclassification is acceptable
Cache at temperature=0 only; cache key = hash(model + system + user_message)
Set separate budget caps for non-critical (news summarization) vs. critical (user-facing answers) pipelines
Review top-10 most expensive agent calls weekly and redesign them — cost concentrates in a few patterns
Always separate prompt caching metrics from semantic caching metrics — they have different failure modes

template.purpose: Per-call token cost tracking with daily budget enforcement
Claude prompt caching with cache_control header
